name: Waterfall Sol Duc
location: Sol Duc River valley, Olympic National Park, Olympic Peninsula, Washington
falls_structure: Three main cascades splitting around rock island; converging in narrow enclosed canyon
park: Olympic National Park (est. 1938; UNESCO World Heritage Site)
river_source: Seven Lakes Basin, Olympic Mountains interior

geology.type: Accreted oceanic basalt (Olympic Peninsula); distinct from Cascade volcanic geology
geology.formation: Oceanic crust accreted to continent over millions of years; folded and faulted into Olympic massif

geothermal.mechanism: Juan de Fuca plate subduction heating groundwater; same tectonic process as Cascade volcanoes

rainforest_context.moss_coverage: Extensive; covers rocks, fallen trees, tree bases, branches
rainforest_context.acoustic_effect: Moss absorbs high frequencies; canyon roar low-frequency component travels farther through forest

acoustic_profile.mechanism: Canyon walls act as resonance chamber; amplify low frequencies below 500 Hz; extend reverberation time
acoustic_profile.vs_open_falls: More tonal than white-noise (Snoqualmie 246); canyon emphasizes specific frequency bands creating 'roar' character
acoustic_profile.audibility: Canyon roar audible hundreds of meters along approach trail; announces falls before visible

indigenous_context.note: Sol Duc valley supported salmon runs (Chinook, Coho, Steelhead); hot springs area used by indigenous peoples; Olympic NP establishment (1938) displaced/restricted indigenous access

roosevelt_elk.significance: Largest Roosevelt Elk population in US; primary game species for Olympic Peninsula peoples; acoustically significant (bugling in rainforest near falls)

theory_nodes.concept: Canyon as resonance chamber: Sol Duc's enclosed basalt canyon amplifies low frequencies (below 500 Hz) and extends reverberation time compared to open-air waterfalls. The canyon geometry is what distinguishes this entry from Snoqualmie (white-noise/open) and river current (distributed/polyrhythm).

Rainforest and canyon as acoustic complements: moss absorbs high frequencies (leaving low), canyon amplifies low frequencies (compensating for forest absorption). The result: the low-frequency canyon roar propagates effectively through the temperate rainforest environment.
theory_nodes.description: Forest and canyon working together acoustically.

theory_nodes.concept: Olympic vs. Cascade geology: the waterfall arc moves from Cascade volcanic geology (245 river, 246 Snoqualmie on Columbia River Basalt) to Olympic accreted oceanic basalt (247 Sol Duc, 248 Hoh). Two different geological origins; two different acoustic and ecological contexts.
theory_nodes.description: Geological transition within the waterfall arc.

cross_references.relationship: The previous waterfall. Snoqualmie: open, white-noise, culturally saturated, 268 ft. Sol Duc: enclosed, resonant, remote wilderness, 48 ft. Two modes of waterfall sound.
The next entry in the Olympic Peninsula arc. Falls → precipitation. Same ecosystem region, different hydrological mode.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=7 + E=8. Canyon resonance meets recording studio resonance. Sol Duc's enclosed basalt canyon amplifies and sustains roar; AIC's studio-recorded guitar tones are thick, warm, deeply resonant. Both use enclosed space as acoustic amplifier. Dirt and Sol Duc: two resonance chambers, one natural, one built.
